Facts.text: Peace deal is signed between Holy Roman Emperor Henry II and the pagan Wends (Slavs)
Pope Clement V excommunicates Venice and its population after Venice seized the papal city of Ferrara
Pope John XXII issues the papal bull "In Agro Dominico," condemning the writings of German mystic Meister Eckhart as heretical
Battle of the Thirty: 30 English and 30 Breton knights and squires square off using swords, maces, lances and daggers - considered one of the most chivalrous battles in history
English nobleman Robert Devereux becomes Lord Lieutenant of Ireland
The first English child born in Canada at Cuper's Cove, Newfoundland to Nicholas Guy
Sixth Patriarch of Moscow and All Russia, Joseph, takes office an unusual 18 months after his predecessor passed away
Tekle Haymanot I takes the throne as Emperor of Ethiopia, upon the retirement and/or abdication of his father, Iyasu I, at 21
James Francis Edward Stuart, Prince of Wales, and pretender to the Great British throne James III flees to Dunkirk after failed invasion attempt
Dike at Hardinxveld breaks, flooding Alblasserwaard in the Netherlands
Spain loses Menorca and Gibraltar to Britain under the Treaty of Utrecht
France and Spain sign the Treaty of Madrid
The modern shoelace with an aglet patented in England by Harvey Kennedy
Denmark and Sweden form a neutrality compact
First Mormon temple is dedicated in Kirtland, Ohio
Mexican Army executes over 425 Texian Army prisoners of war in Goliad, Republic of Texas
First US steam fire engine is tested in New York City
John Parker Paynard originates medicated adhesive plaster
Joseph Couch patents steam-powered percussion rock drill
M. L. Byrn patents a "covered gimlet screw with a 'T' handle," more popularly known as a corkscrew
Battle of Spanish Fort: Union forces led by E.R.S. Canby lay siege to the heavily fortified Confederate Spanish Fort in Baldwin County, Alabama
American Andrew Rankin patents the urinal
Lake Ontario Shore Railroad Company is organized in Oswego, New York
First international rugby union match, Scotland beats England 1-0 at Raeburn Place, Edinburgh
Longest championship fight (136 rounds)
Rioting takes place in Basingstoke, Hampshire in England, to protest against the Salvation Army's daily vociferous promotion of rigid Temperance
First long-distance telephone call, Boston to New York
A tornado strikes Louisville, Kentucky, killing 76 and injuring 200.
Recognising that the war in South Africa is going to take a major commitment, Parliament passes the War Loan Act, calling for £35 million to support the fight against the Boers.
Founding of the Alpine Club of Canada in Winnipeg, Manitoba
Fire during a barn-dance in Ököritófülpös, Hungary, kills 312
US First Lady Helen Herron Taft and the Viscountess Chinda, wife of the Japanese Ambassador, plant two Yoshino cherry trees on the bank of the Tidal Basin, Washington, D.C.
First successful non-direct blood transfusion is performed by Dr. Albert Hustin in Brussels
Moldova and Bessarabia join Romania
Hermann Muller becomes German chancellor (SPD)
Canada recognizes USSR
New French government of Poincaré begins
KGB-AM in San Diego CA begins radio transmissions
1st US radio broadcast from a ship at sea
John McGraw says night baseball will not catch on
De Bataven soccer team forms in Gendt
Farm Credit Administration (US) authorized
Japan leaves League of Nations
Polythene discovered by Reginald Gibson & Eric William Fawcett
WOS-AM in Jefferson City Missouri goes off the air
Feijenoord Stadion, home ground of Dutch football club Feyenoord Rotterdam and nicknamed De Kuip opens after 2 years in construction
The Battle of Taierzhuang takes place.
Britain leases defense bases in Trinidad to US for 99 years
Yugoslavian coup gets rid of pro-German Prince Paul
-28] Allies raid German submarine base in St Nazaire
Japan forces Java to use "Tokyo time" 1½ hour forward

WWII: Dutch resistance fighters burn and bomb the Amsterdam civil registry office in effort to destroy records and prevent the Nazis from identifying Jews and others marked for persecution; about 15% of records destroyed and 12 of the 19 known participants were tried and executed by the Nazi occupiers
WWII: US begins assault on Fondouk Pass, Tunisia
2,000 Jews are murdered in Kaunas Lithuania
40 Jewish policemen in Riga, Latvia, ghetto are shot by the Gestapo
Children's Aktion-Nazis collect all the Jewish children of Lovno
DePaul beats Bowling Green for NIT title
US 20th Army corps captures Wiesbaden
World War II: Operation Starvation, the aerial mining of Japan's ports and waterways begins.
The Second Congress of the Workers' Party of North Korea is convened
Netherlands recognizes People's Republic of China
WHAS TV channel 11 in Louisville, Kentucky (CBS) begins broadcasting
Sun Records of Memphis begins releasing records
21 die in a train crash in Conneaut, Ohio
9th Tony Awards: "The Desperate Hours" (play) & "The Pajama Game" (musical) win
WPRI TV channel 12 in Providence, Rhode Island (ABC) begins broadcasting
French commandos land in Algeria
CBS Labs announce new stereophonic records
Havana Hilton opens in Cuba, later HQ for Fidel Castro
Black demonstrators in Charleston staged ride-ins on street cars
Failed assassination attempt on King Saif al-Islam Achmad of Yemen
Ann Jellicoe's play "Knack" premieres in London
Archbishop Rummel ends race segregation in New Orleans Catholic schools
Richard Beeching issues a report known as the "Beeching Axe," calling for significant cuts to the UK rail network
Great Train Robbers sentenced to a total of 307 years behind bars
The Great Alaska Earthquake (9.2 magnitude) and resulting tsunami kill 139 people in the largest US earthquake and second largest ever recorded
UN troops arrive in Cyprus
Japanese Trade & Cultural Center (Japan Center) dedicated in San Francisco
Black Academy of Arts & Letters forms in Boston
Launch of Mariner 7, flies 2,190-mi above southern Mars
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
David Heneker and John Taylor's musical "Charlie Girl" close at the Adelphi Theatre, London, after 2,202 performances
The Alliance Party of Northern Ireland (APNI) holds its first Annual Conference in the Ulster Hall in Belfast
Adolph Rupp retires after 42 years of coaching University of Kentucky
Ulster Vanguard organise industrial strike against the imposition of direct rule on Northern Ireland by Westminster
Venera 8 launches to explore Venus
Wyoming officially names "Curt Gowdy State Park", in honor of the nationally recognized broadcaster
Dennis Amiss out for 99 v Pakistan, 3rd 99 in Test Cricket
Washington, D.C. underground Metro opens
The Rutles mockumentary "All You Need is Cash" is shown on British TV
US Supreme Court rules 8-1 that cops can't randomly stop cars
Elevator in Vaal Reefs gold mine, South Africa plunges more than 1900m killing all 23 miners aboard
Norwegian oil platform Alexander Kielland collapses in the North Sea, killing 123 of its crew of 212
Imran takes 14-116 for cricket match v Sri Lanka at Lahore
Randy Holt sets Wash Cap record of 34 penalty minutes
Beginning of "tanker war": over the next 9 months, 44 ships, including Iranian, Iraqi, Saudi Arabian and Kuwaiti tankers, are attacked by Iraqi or Iranian warplanes or damaged by mines
Bus transporting school children from Vorentoe High School plunges into Westdene Dam, Johannesburg, South Africa, killing 42
Disney-MGM Studio Tour groundbreaking
Ice Dance Championship at Budapest won by Bestemianova & Bukin (URS)
Ice Pairs Championship at Budapest won by E Valova & O Vasiliev (URS)
Ok-Hee Ku wins Standard Register Turquoise Classic Golf Tournament
1st African American soap opera, "Generations" premieres on NBC-TV
Delhi beat Bengal by innings & 210 to win Cricket's Ranji Trophy
Bus accidentally touches high voltage wire in Karagpur India; 21 die
US begins broadcasting TV Martí to Cuba in an effort to bridge the information blackout imposed by the Castro regime
NCAA bans U of Minn football team from postseason play in 1992
New Kid Donnie Wahlberg, arrested on arson charges in Kentucky
Church in Piedmont Alabama collapses in tornado, 19 killed
Ice Dance Championship at Chiba Japan won by Gritschuk & Platov (RUS)
Ice Pairs Championship at Chiba won by Shishkova/Vadim Naumov (RUS)
The Eurofighter takes its first flight in Manching, Germany
Two-month ceasefire agreed by Government of Sudan in its civil war, to treat health conditions such as Guinea worm and river blindness, lasts six months, the longest-ever health ceasefire
Chicago Bulls - Atlanta Hawks matchup at Georgia Dome in Atlanta, draws a crowd of 62,046, largest in any game in NBA history; Bulls win, 89-74
Phillips explosion kills 1 and injures 71 in Pasadena, Texas.
Passover Massacre: Suicide bomber kills 29 people in Netanya, Israel
HMS Scylla, a decommissioned Leander class frigate, is sunk as an artificial reef off Cornwall, the first of its kind in Europe.
United Nations Commission on Human Rights holds its final meeting before it is replaced by the Human Rights Council
Situ Gintung, an artificial lake in Indonesia, fails, killing at least 99 people
On This Day launches a sister site in Spanish, Hoy en la Historia
12 people are killed in the Philippines after a mini-tornado causes a boat to capsize
UN General Assembly condemns Russia's annexation of Crimea
Russia's Soyuz TMA-16M launches to deliver three crew members to the international space station to research the long-term effects of micro gravity
Suicide bomb kills more than 70 people at a park in Lahore, Pakistan, Taliban connected Jamaat-ul-Ahrar claim responsibility

British indie-rock duo "Her's" - English guitarist-singer Stephen Fitzpatrick (24) and Norwegian bassist Audun Laading (25), and American manager Trevor Engelbrektson (37) killed in car crash in Arizona
Facebook bans white nationalism and white supremacy following criticism that Christchurch terrorist able to live-stream his attack
UK Prime Minister Theresa May promises to stand down if parliament accepts her Brexit plan

US Special Council Robert S. Mueller writes a letter to US Attorney William Barr regarding Barr's summary of the Mueller Report stating Barr's letter "did not fully capture the context, nature, and substance" of the findings. "There is now public confusion about critical aspects of the results of our investigation. This threatens to undermine a central purpose for which the Department appointed the Special Counsel: to assure full public confidence in the outcome of the investigations."
114 people including children killed in one day by armed forces in Myanmar, with more than 420 killed since protests began against the military coup
Iran and China sign major agreement guaranteeing Chinese investment of $400 billion and Iranian oil supply in return in Tehran
Militants attack the town of Palma, northern Mozambique, killing dozens in an escalation of violence in the area
China announces Shanghai will be locked down in two stages over nine days affecting 25 million people to carry out COVID-19 testing
El Salvador's parliament declares a state of emergency after 62 gang killings in one day
At least 40 people are killed and 28 injured in a fire at a migrant detention center in Ciudad Juárez on the Mexican US border, amid large numbers of migrants gathering on the Mexican border
Convicted rapist fakes his own death by pretending to set himself alight (in reality kills someone else) to escape from prison in South Africa, sparking large manhunt
Female shooter kills three children and three adults at private Christian school Convent Elementary, in Nashville, Tennessee, before being shot and killed - 19th US school shooting in three months
Germany experiences one of its largest strikes in decades, bringing public transport comes to an almost complete halt, amid calls for higher wages
Humza Yousaf elected leader of Scottish SNP party as first ethnic minority leader of a devolved government and first Muslim to lead a major UK party

Possibility of bases on the Moon becomes greater possibility after 300 billion tonnes of water, stored in glass beads on Moon's surface, identified in lunar soil samples collected by China’s Chang’e-5
World's oldest tartan confirmed as up to 500 years old, after testing on material found 40 years earlier in Glen Affric bog, by experts at National Museums Scotland
Harvard University says it will remove a 19th century book binding of human skin, originally taken without consent from a deceased French mental patient
New analysis confirms climate change is slowing the rotation of Earth due to melting ice caps and will probably delay the next leap second by three years
European space telescope Gaia is shut down after a decade of charting the Milky Way and collecting data on nearly two billion stars and other celestial objects
Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. announces a major restructure of the US Department of Health, including laying off 33 % of its workforce, about 10,000 employees
32nd predicted perihelion passage of Halley's Comet
SpaniardJuan Ponce de Leónand his expedition first sight Florida
Charles I, King of England, Scotland and Ireland ascends the English throne
English KingCharles IIgrants Royal Charter giving control of Bombay, India to the English East India Company
Charles Watson-Wentworth, 2nd Marquess of Rockingham, becomes Prime Minister of Great Britain
Naval Act of 1794 is signed by PresidentGeorge Washington, authorizing the building of six frigates and establishing a permanent US Navy
Joseph Haydn's oratorio "Die Schopfung" premieres in Vienna
Battle at Horseshoe Bend: GeneralAndrew Jacksondefeats the Red Sticks, part of the Creek Indian tribe near Dadeville, Alabama
Canadian geologistAbraham Gesnerpatents kerosene
American Confederate PresidentJefferson Daviscalls for a day of fasting & prayer
US PresidentAndrew Johnsonvetoes civil rights bill; it later becomes 14th amendment

Typhoid Mary[Mary Mallon] is arrested and returned to quarantine on North Brother Island, New York after spending five years evading health authorities and causing several further outbreaks of typhoid
Charlie Chaplinreceives France's distinguished Legion of Honor
Peter Fraserbecomes Prime Minister of New Zealand after the death of his predecessor Michael Joeseph Savage from cancer
Adolf Hitlersigns Directive 27 (assault on Yugoslavia)
Joe LouisKOs Abe Simon in 6 to retain heavyweight boxing title (NYC)
Blue Ribbon Town (withGroucho Marx) 1st heard on CBS Radio

Transport No. 70 departs Drancy (France) internment camp, taking 1,000 French Jews toAuschwitzextermination camp in Nazi German occupied Poland; 480 killed upon arrival, approximately 152 survive the war
British Prime MinisterWinston Churchillsails to eastern banks of Rhine
Ella Fitzgeraldand Delta Rhythm Boys record theHarold Arlen-Yip Harburg-Billy Rosesong "It's Only a Paper Moon"
GeneralEisenhowerdeclares German defenses on Western Front broken
Just 11 days after being released from prison, jazz singerBillie Holidayplays in front of a sold-out crowd at Carnegie Hall, NYC
Jazz pianistErroll Garnerperforms a solo recital at Cleveland Music Hall, a venue for traditionally classical concerts, in Cleveland, Ohio
Frank Sinatrarecords "I'm a Fool to Want You"
"Singin' in the Rain," a musical comedy film directed byGene Kellyand Stanley Donen and starring Gene Kelly andDebbie Reynolds, premieres at Radio City Music Hall in NYC
Failed assassination attempt of West German ChancellorKonrad Adenauer
Steve McQueenmakes his network TV debut (Goodyear Playhouse, episode "The Chivington Raid")
29th Academy Awards: "Around World in 80 Days",Ingrid Bergman,Yul Brynnerwin
Nikita Khrushchevbecomes Soviet Premier as well as First Secretary of the Communist Party
NHL Montreal Canadiens goaltenderJacques Planteties record winning 6th NHL Vezina trophy
Historical film "The Leopard" directed by Luchino Visconti, starringBurt Lancaster,Alain Delon, and Claudia Cardinale premieres in Rome, based on the novel by Giuseppe Tomasi di Lampedusa
Suhartoofficially succeedsSukarnoas president of Indonesia
Ringo Starrreleases his 1st solo album "Sentimental Journey", a collection of pre-rock standards

45th Academy Awards: "TheGodfather",Marlon Brando& Liza Minnelli win; Brando declines the Oscar for Best Actor to protest Hollywood’s portrayal of Native Americans in film, in a speech by activist Sacheen Littlefeather
Jerry Garcia(Grateful Dead) stopped for speeding and found in possession of LSD
American tennis starChris Evertwins her 4th and final WTA Tour Championship 2–6, 6–1, 6–1 against England's Sue Barker at Madison Square Garden, NYC
"Blizzard of Ozz", the debut solo album by English rock singerOzzy Osbourne, is released in the United States
John Lennon's single "Watching the Wheels" released posthumously in UK
Larry Holmesbeats Lucien Rodriguez in 12 for heavyweight boxing title
Neil Simon's "Brighton Beach Memoirs" premieres in NYC
Andrew Lloyd Webberand Richard Stilgoe's rock musical "Starlight Express" opens at the Apollo Victoria Theatre in London's West End
American actorBilly Dee Williamsreceives a star on Hollywood Walk of Fame

Professional wrestlerHulk Hoganacquiesces to put interviewerRichard Belzerin a hold, resulting in Belzer passing out, hitting his head on the floor, and requiring 9 stitches; Belzer sues Hogan for $5 million in damages for personal injury, they settle out of court, and Belzer buys a home in France
Chadian PresidentHissène Habré's troops reconquer Faya Largeau in Chad
Men's Figure Skating Champions in Budapest won byBrian Boitano(USA)
Scotty Bowmaninducted into the Hockey Hall of Fame as a "builder" of the game
Columbia Records releasesBruce Springsteen's 9th and 10th studio albums - "Human Touch" and "Lucky Town" - on the same day; musicians other than the E-Street Band are used
Jiang Zeminappointed President of the People's Republic of China
"Back for Good" single released by British boy bandTake That, reaches No. 1 in 31 countries
67th Academy Awards: "Forrest Gump",Jessica Lange&Tom Hankswin
Martin Luther King's son meetsJames Earl Ray, his father's killer
TV medical drama "Grey's Anatomy" created byShonda RhimesstarringEllen PompeoandSandra Ohdebuts on ABC
Danielle Steel’s novel “Betrayal” is published
Former president of the GambiaYahya Jammehstole almost 1 billion from his country before his exile in 2017 according to a corruption report
Indian Prime MinisterNarendra Modiannounces the country is now a "space power" after successfully shooting down a satellite from space in a ballistic missile test
$2.2 trillion stimulus package, largest in US history, signed into law by PresidentDonald Trumpsaying "I never signed anything with a 'T' on it"
UK Prime MinisterBoris Johnsonannounces he has contracted COVID-19 but will continue to lead the country "thanks to the wizardry of modern technology" while in self-isolation
94th Academy Awards: "CODA" wins best film, best directorJane Campion, best actressJessica Chastain, best actorWill Smithwho also controversially slaps hostChris Rocklive on stage
Duke andDuchess of Cambridgeend an eight-day tour of Belize, Jamaica and the Bahamas that highlighted issues of decolonization and calls for slave reparations
Israeli PMBenjamin Netanyahuannounces pause of key part of controversial plans to overhaul the justice system after massive protests and the country's largest trade union goes on strike
The Rosetta Stone and others like it are inscribed with the Decree of Memphis proclaiming the rule of the King of Ptolemaic Egypt, Ptolemy V
583 die in aviation's worst ever disaster when two Boeing 747s collide at Tenerife airport in Spain
Mount St. Helens becomes active after 123 years
Robert II of France [the Pious, the Wise], King of the Franks (996-1031), born in Orléans, France (d. 1031)
Antonio Squarcialupi [Giovanni], Italian composer and organist (Cathedral of Santa Maria del Fiore, 1432-80), born in Florence, Republic of Florence (d. 1480)
Eberhard Bronchorst, Dutch lawyer, born in Deventer, Netherlands (d. 1627)
Stephen Fox, English politician, born in Farley, Wiltshire (d. 1716)
Benjamin Neukirch, German poet (Herrn von Hofmannswaldau), born in Silesia, Poland (d. 1729)
Francis II Rákóczi, Hungarian nobleman and leader of the Hungarian uprising against the Habsburg, born in Borsi, Royal Hungary (d. 1735)
Johann Ernst Eberlin, German composer, born in Jettingen-Scheppach, Electorate of Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1762)
Joseph Abaco, Italian composer, born in Brussels, Spanish Netherlands (d. 1805)
Claude Bourgelat, French pioneering veterinary surgeon, started world's first veterinary school, born in Lyon, France (d. 1779)
Francesco Antonio Zaccaria, Italian theologian and historian, born in Venice (d. 1795)
James Madison Sr., American  tobacco farmer, colonel, politician, and father of 4th President James Madison, born in Orange County, Virginia (d. 1801)
Thomas Tyrwhitt, English classical scholar and critic, born in London (d. 1786)
Augustin Ullinger, German composer, born in Ranoldsberg, Upper Bavaria, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1781)
Carlo Buonaparte, Corsican attorney and father of emperor Napoleon, born in Ajaccio, Corsica, Republic of Genoa (d. 1785)
Michael Bruce, Scottish poet (Elegy written in Spring), born in Portmoak, Kinross-shire, Scotland (d. 1767)
Richard John Samuel Stevens, English composer, born in London, (d. 1837)
Ishmail Spicer, American publisher, music teacher, and composer, born in Bozrah, Connecticut (d. 1832)
Marie-Jean Auguste Vestris, French ballet dancer, born in Paris (d. 1842)
Franz Xaver von Baader, German philosopher and theologist, born in Munich (d. 1841)
Giovanni Liverati, Italian composer, born in Bologna, Papal States (d. 1846)
August Leopold Crelle, German inventor and mathematician (1st Prussian Railway), born in Eichwerder, Brandenburg, Germany (d. 1855)
Alexander Csoma de Kőrös, Hungarian Tibetan scholar (first Tibetan-English Dictionary), born in Kőrös, Transylvania (d. 1842)
Jonathan Jennings, American politician (Governor of Indiana, 1816-22; Congressman for Indiana, 1822-31), born in Readington, New Jersey (d. 1834)
Louis XVII Charles, King of France (1793-95), born in Palace of Versailles, France (d. 1795)
Alfred Victor, Comte de Vigny, French musketeer and writer (Moise, Chatterton), born in Loches, France (d. 1863)
Georges Eugene, Baron Haussmann, French administrator and architect, born in Paris (d. 1891)
Adolf Glassbrenner, German satirical writer, born in Berlin (d. 1876)
William Hepworth Thompson, English classical scholar and Master of Trinity College, Cambridge, born in York, England (d. 1886)
Nathaniel Currier, American lithographer (Currier & Ives), born in Roxbury, Massachusetts (d. 1888)
George Elvey, British organist and composer, born in Canterbury, England (d. 1893)
Karl Wilhelm von Nägeli, Swiss botanist (famous for his work on plant cells), born in Kilchberg, Switzerland (d. 1891)
Jacob Axel Josephson, Swedish composer, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1880)
Samuel Kosciusko Zook, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Tredyffrin Township, Pennsylvania (d. 1863)
Adolphus Washington Greely, American Arctic explorer and recipient of the Medal of Honor, born in Newburyport, Massachusetts (d. 1935)
Otto Wallach, German chemist (Nobel 1910), born in Königsberg, Germany (d. 1931)
Ruperto Chapí, Spanish composer, born in Villena, Spain (d. 1909)
Alfred Ewing, Scottish Physicist (magnetic properties of metals, hysteresis), born in Dundee, Angus, Scotland (d. 1935)
Karl Pearson, British statistician (leading founder of the modern field of statistics), born in London (d. 1936)
Peter C. Lutkin, American composer, born in Thomp­son­ville, Wis­con­sin (d. 1931)
George Giffen, Australian cricket all-rounder (31 Tests, 1 x 100, 6 x 50, HS 161, 103 wickets, BB 7/117; SA CA), born in Adelaide, Australia (d. 1927)
Frank Frost Abbott, American classical scholar, born in Redding, Connecticut (d. 1924)
Edyth Walker, American opera singer, born in Hopewell, New York (d. 1950)
Patty Smith Hill, American composer, teacher and songwriter ("Happy Birthday To You"), born in Anchorage, Kentucky (d. 1946)
James McNeill, Irish politician, 2nd Governor-General of the Irish Free State, born in Glenarm, County Antrim, Ireland (d. 1938)
Heinrich Mann, German novelist and essayist (Professor Unrat); brother of Thomas, born in the Free Imperial City of Lübeck (d. 1950)
Piet Aalberse, Dutch minister of Labor (1918-25), born in Leiden, Netherlands (d. 1948)
Miller Huggins, American Baseball Hall of Fame manager (World Series 1923, 27, 28; 6 x AL pennants NY Yankees), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1929)
Edward Steichen, Luxembourgish-American photographer (The Family of Man), born in Bivange, Luxembourg (d. 1973)
Jan Kunc, Czech composer (Czech State Anthem), born in Doubravice nad Svitavou, Margraviate of Moravia, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1976)
Marie Under, Estonian author and poet, born in Tallinn, Russian Empire (d. 1980)
Ludwig Mies van der Rohe, German-American architect (November Group), born in Aachen, Germany (d. 1969)
Sergey Kirov [Kostrikov], Russian Bolshevik leader and politician (First Secretary (Mayor) of Leningrad, 1926-34), born in Urzhum, Russia (d. 1934)
Agnes Windeck, German actress (A Time to Love and a Time to Die), born in Hamburg, Germany (d. 1975)
George Hearne, South African cricket batsman (3 Tests; Western Province), born in Catford, England (d. 1978)
Yakup Kadri Karaosmanoglu, Turkish writer and translator, born in Cairo, Egypt (d. 1974)
Ferde Grofé, American pianist, composer (Grand Canyon Suite), and arranger (Gershwin's Rhapsody in Blue), born in New York City (d. 1972)
Thorne Smith, American sci-fi author (Topper, Rain in the Doorway, Stray Lamb), born in Annapolis, Maryland (d. 1934)
Karl Mannheim, Hungarian sociologist and writer (Ideology and Utopia; Diagnosis of Our Time), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1947)
William Harrigan, American actor (Invisible Man), born in New York City (d. 1966)
Carlo Mierendorff, German politician and anti-fascist, born in Grossenhain, Germany (d. 1943)
Douglas R. Hartree, English mathematician and physicist, born in Cambridge, England (d. 1958)
Effa Manley, American Baseball HOF executive (co-owner NgL Newark Eagles), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1981)
Albert Henneberg, Swedish composer, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1991)
Erich Ollenhauer, German politician, leader of the Social Democratic Party of Germany (1952-63), born in Madgeburg, Germany (d. 1963)
Charles Bryant Lang Jr., American cinematographer, born in Bluff, Utah (d. 1998)
Mary Armour, Scottish painter, born in Blantyre (d. 2000)
Hal Kemp American jazz alto saxophonist, clarinetist, arranger and sweet jazz bandleader, born in Marion, Alabama (d. 1940)
Elsie MacGill, Canadian aeronautical engineer, 1st female in Canada to get bachelor's degree in electrical engineering, born in Vancouver (d. 1980)
John Fleming (Jack) Brock, South African nutritional scientist, physician and a professor in the Department of Medicine at the University of Cape Town, born in Port Elizabeth, South Africa (d. 1983)
Leroy Carr, American blues piano player and singer-songwriter ("How Long, How Long Blues"), born in Nashville, Tennessee (d. 1935)
Charles "Pee Wee" Russell, American jazz clarinet and saxophone player, born in Maplewood, Missouri (d. 1969)
Claude Stroud, American actor (Breakfast at Tiffany's; Ted Knight Show; Duke), born in Kaufman, Texas (d. 1985)
Mary Treen (née Summers), American character actress (It's a Wonderful Life; Kitty Foyle; The Joey Bishop Show), born in St Louis, Missouri (d. 1989)
Jacques [Izaak] den Haan, Dutch writer (Dangerous Book), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1982)
Ben Webster, American jazz tenor saxophonist, born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 1973)
Golo Mann, German-American historian (Antisemitism), born in Munich (d. 1994)
Ai Qing, Chinese poet, born in Jinhua, Zhejiang province (d. 1996)
Frank Smailes, English cricket all-rounder (1 Test; 8 County C'ships, Yorkshire), born in Ripley, North Yorkshire, England (d. 1970)
John Pierce, American engineer (1st communications satellite Telstar 1), born in Des Moines, Iowa (d. 2002)
Rudi Ball, German-Jewish ice hockey star (Olympic bronze 1932), born in Berlin (d. 1975)
Reuel Lahmer, Canadian-American organist and composer ("It Is Good To Give Thanks"), born in Maple, Ontario (d. after 1982)
Robert Watson Hughes, Scottish-Australian composer, born in Levan, Inverclyde, Scotland (d. 2007)
Godfrey Turner, British composer, born in Manchester, England (d. 1948)
June Beebe, American golfer (Western Open 1931, 33), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2003)
Theodor Dannecker, SS officer and an associate of Adolf Eichmann, born in Tübingen, Germany (d. 1945)
Budd Schulberg, American novelist and screenwriter (On the Waterfront), born in New York City (d. 2009)
Richard Denning [Louis Denninger Jr.], American actor (An Affair to Remember, The Black Scorpion), born in Poughkeepsie, New York (d. 1998)
Snooky Lanson [Roy Landman], American singer (Your Hit Parade; 5 Star Jubilee), born in Memphis, Tennessee (d. 1990)
Robert Lockwood Jr., American blues guitarist, born in Turkey Scratch, Arkansas (d. 2006)
Johnny Drake, American football running back who was First-team All-Pro 1940; 3 × NFL All-Star, Cleveland Rams, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 1973)
Cyrus Vance, US Secretary of State (1977-80), born in Clarksburg, West Virginia (d. 2002)
Harry West, Irish politician, leader of the Ulster Unionist Party (1974-79), born in Enniskillen, Ireland (d. 2004)
Julian Amery, British conservative politician and minister, born in London (d. 1996)
Simon van Collem, Dutch journalist and TV host (Amsterdamned), born in Amsterdam (d. 1989)
Carl-Henrik Norin Swedish jazz saxophonist, and composer ("Mississippi Mood"), born in Västerås, Sweden (d. 1967)
Richard Hayman, American harmonica player ("Ruby"), arranger, and orchestra leader (Boston Pops; St. Louis Pops; Grand Rapids Pops), born in Cambridge, Massachusetts (d. 2014)
Robin Jacques, English illustrator, born in Chelsea (d. 1995)
Harold Nicholas, American dancer known as one of the world's greatest dancers (Nicholas Brothers), born in Winston-Salem, North Carolina (d. 2000)
Phil Chess [Fiszel Czyż], Polish-American record company executive (Chess Records), and jazz record producer, born in Motal, Poland (d. 2016)
Tom Bevill, American Democratic fifteen-term U.S. congressman, born in Townley, Alabama (d. 2005)
Margaret Stacey, English sociologist who helped shape the distinctively British genre of empirical sociology, born in London (d. 2004)
Stefan Wul, French sci-fi author (The Temple of the Past), born in Paris (d. 2003)
Louis Simpson, Jamaican-American poet (Good News of Death), born in Kingston, Jamaica (d. 2012)
Shūsaku Endō, Japanese writer (Sea & Poison, Silence), born in Tokyo, Japan (d. 1996)
Victor Hochhauser, British impresario (Israeli Philharmonic Orchestra), born in Košice, Czechoslovakia (d. 2019)
